title: New Relic API keys should not be disclosed
description: In most cases, trust boundaries are violated when a secret is exposed in a source code repository or an uncontrolled deployment environment. Unintended people who don’t need to know the secret might get access to it. They might then be able to use it to gain unwanted access to associated services or resources.
recommendation: Review and fix according to the rule guidelines.

checks.message: New Relic User API Key detected (NRAK- prefix). This key grants full API access to your New Relic account, allowing complete control over applications, infrastructure monitoring, alerts, dashboards, and account settings. Exposed keys enable unauthorized users to view sensitive performance data, modify monitoring configurations, access customer information, and potentially disrupt critical monitoring services. Revoke this key immediately and use environment variables or secrets management systems.

New Relic License Key detected (40-character hex string). License keys are used to report data to New Relic APM, Browser, Mobile, and Infrastructure. Exposed license keys allow attackers to send fraudulent data to your account, consume your data ingest quota, inflate costs, and potentially inject malicious metrics that could trigger false alerts or mask real issues. Store license keys securely and rotate immediately if exposed.

New Relic Insights Insert Key detected (NRII- prefix). This key allows inserting custom events and metrics into New Relic Insights. Exposed keys enable attackers to inject false data, manipulate dashboards, create misleading analytics, consume your data quota, and potentially mask security incidents by flooding your account with fake events. Rotate this key immediately and restrict access to authorized systems only.

New Relic Browser Key detected (NRJS- prefix). Browser keys are used by the New Relic Browser agent to report frontend performance data. While less critical than API keys, exposed browser keys can still allow attackers to pollute your Browser monitoring data, consume your data quota, and potentially gather information about your application's frontend architecture. Consider rotating this key and implementing proper key management.

New Relic API key or token variable detected in configuration. Hardcoded New Relic credentials in source code expose sensitive monitoring keys that control access to application performance data, infrastructure metrics, and account settings. This allows unauthorized access to view production metrics, modify alerts, access error traces, and potentially disrupt monitoring operations. Use environment variables or secure secrets management instead.
